title: Female labor force participation rate
subtitle: Share of the female working-age population (ages 15 and over) who are economically active (employed or unemployed).
note: All figures correspond to [modeled ILO estimates](#dod:ilo-modelled-estimates). Employment is defined in line with the [13th ICLS](#dod:13-icls) and includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming.
caption:
Female labor force participation rate

Share of the female working-age population (ages 15 and over) who are economically active (employed or unemployed).

This choropleth world map displays modeled estimates for the female labor force participation rate in 2025. The legend ranges from 0% to 70%, using a color scale that transitions from pale yellow for the lowest rates to dark blue for the highest. The darkest blue shades, indicating participation rates above 60% and approaching 70%, are visible in specific countries including Burundi, Rwanda, Tanzania, and Madagascar in East Africa, as well as Laos in Southeast Asia. Large portions of Sub-Saharan Africa, including Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, appear in medium-to-dark blue bands, generally falling between 40% and 60%. In contrast, a broad band of countries across North Africa and the Middle East, including Saudi Arabia, Iraq, and Algeria, sits in the lightest yellow shades, corresponding to rates below 20% or near 30%. South Asian nations like India and Pakistan also appear in these lighter yellow bands. North America, Australia, and most of Europe display mid-range teal and blue shades, clustering roughly between 40% and 60%. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. All figures correspond to modeled ILO estimates, where employment is defined in line with the 13th ICLS and includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming. The visual distribution highlights a concentration of the highest participation rates in parts of East Africa and Southeast Asia, while the lowest rates are clustered across North Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia.
